fact: August 12 is exactly 224 days into the year, which means there are only 141 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is peak cricket chirping season - you can actually tell the temperature by counting cricket chirps for 14 seconds and adding 40!
The sun rises around 6 AM and sets around 8 PM in most of North America on this date, giving you about 14 hours of daylight to play outside.
August 12 falls smack in the middle of Leo season, when the zodiac lion is roaring loudest in the sky.
Monarch butterflies are getting seriously restless around this time in North America - they're starting to feel the urge to fly thousands of miles south to Mexico!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, August 12 is deep winter, and some penguins are huddling together in groups of thousands to stay warm.
Corn mazes start opening around this date in many places because the corn stalks have grown tall enough to get completely lost in!
The moon can look extra orange or red when it rises on summer evenings like this because you're looking at it through more of Earth's thick, dusty atmosphere.
August 12 is roughly 54 days after the summer solstice, which means daylight is shrinking by about 2-3 minutes every single day in the north.
Fireflies are having their last big light shows of the year around this time - after August, most of them will stop flashing and disappear until next summer.
Spider webs covered in morning dew become super visible on August mornings because the air is getting just cool enough at night to create heavy dew drops.
In many northern places, this is when lakes and rivers hit their warmest temperatures of the entire year - perfect for swimming without turning into a popsicle!
